subject: ap-world-history
theme: Globalization, 1900–present: The post-Cold War era produces unprecedented economic and cultural integration, generating both benefits and profound inequalities, and new forms of resistance.

core_concepts.title: Economic Globalization: Institutions and Mechanisms
core_concepts.summary: Post-Cold War economic globalization was structured by institutions established at Bretton Woods (1944) and expanded afterward: the IMF (stabilizing currencies and providing crisis loans), the World Bank (development finance), and GATT → WTO (1995, trade rules). Neoliberal economic policies — free markets, deregulation, privatization, reduced government spending — became dominant through the 'Washington Consensus' promoted by the US and international financial institutions. Trade as a share of global GDP rose from 8% (1945) to 25% (2000s). Multinational corporations integrated global supply chains, shifting manufacturing to low-wage nations (Mexico, China, Bangladesh, Vietnam).

core_concepts.title: China's Economic Rise
core_concepts.summary: China's transformation from poor agrarian nation to world's second-largest economy represents one of history's most dramatic economic changes. Deng Xiaoping's reforms (1978) established Special Economic Zones attracting foreign investment, allowed market pricing, and integrated China into global supply chains. By 2010 China had become the world's largest manufacturing exporter and the primary source of poverty reduction in global development statistics. China's rise challenged US economic dominance and created new patterns of South-South trade, investment, and infrastructure development (Belt and Road Initiative, 2013). It also generated intense US-China trade and technology rivalry.

core_concepts.title: Inequality and Globalization's Distributional Effects
core_concepts.summary: Globalization's effects on poverty and inequality are paradoxical. Extreme poverty (below $1.90/day) fell dramatically — from 1.9 billion (1990) to 700 million (2015), largely due to China's growth. However, income inequality within most nations increased sharply. The 'race to the bottom' — countries competing for investment by lowering wages and environmental standards — kept manufacturing wages suppressed. The Washington Consensus's structural adjustment programs required African and Latin American nations to cut social spending as conditions for loans, deteriorating public services. Dependency theory (Wallerstein, world-systems) argues globalization reproduces colonial-era core-periphery extraction.

core_concepts.title: Technology, Communication, and Globalization
core_concepts.summary: The internet and mobile communications fundamentally transformed globalization's cultural and economic dimensions. The World Wide Web (1991) enabled real-time global communication, e-commerce, and the outsourcing of knowledge work. Mobile banking (M-Pesa) reached unbanked populations in sub-Saharan Africa. Social media enabled both transnational activism (Arab Spring, 2010–12) and authoritarian surveillance. The digital divide — unequal internet access between wealthy and poor nations — threatened to deepen existing inequalities. Artificial intelligence and automation continued displacing workers in developing-country manufacturing, creating new pressures on countries pursuing export-led development.

core_concepts.title: Resistance to Globalization
core_concepts.summary: Economic globalization generated diverse forms of resistance. The anti-globalization movement — dramatically demonstrated at the 1999 Seattle WTO protests — united labor unions, environmentalists, and development advocates criticizing free trade's effects on workers, the environment, and developing nations. The Zapatista uprising (1994, Mexico) explicitly protested NAFTA. Religious fundamentalism — Islamic, Hindu, Christian, Jewish — emerged in part as a reaction to globalization's perceived cultural and moral threats. Nationalist and populist political movements (Brexit, Trump's election) drew support from deindustrialized communities experiencing job losses from trade and automation.

core_concepts.title: Global Environmental Challenges
core_concepts.summary: Industrial globalization intensified pressure on the natural environment. Climate change — caused by centuries of fossil fuel combustion — emerged as the defining global challenge: rising temperatures, sea-level rise, extreme weather events, and ocean acidification. International climate agreements attempted to coordinate responses: Kyoto Protocol (1997, binding targets for developed nations); Paris Agreement (2015, voluntary nationally determined contributions from all nations). The sovereignty-effectiveness tension — states' reluctance to accept binding international constraints on their economies — has limited climate action's ambition. Environmental costs of globalization are disproportionately borne by poor nations and communities with least historical responsibility for emissions.

key_terms.definition: Economic ideology advocating free markets, deregulation, privatization, and reduced government spending; became globally dominant policy framework through the Washington Consensus in the 1980s–1990s.
Set of economic policy prescriptions (austerity, privatization, trade liberalization, deregulation) promoted by the IMF and World Bank as conditions for development loans in the 1980s–1990s.
International body (1995) that replaced GATT to govern global trade rules and resolve trade disputes; promotes trade liberalization but is criticized for favoring wealthy-nation interests.
IMF and World Bank loan conditions requiring developing nations to cut government spending, privatize state enterprises, and liberalize trade — widely criticized for devastating social services.
Dynamic where nations compete for multinational investment by lowering wages, environmental standards, and labor protections — creating a competitive spiral of deteriorating conditions.
Areas in China (and elsewhere) with special regulations attracting foreign investment — lower taxes, flexible labor rules — key mechanism of China's export-led industrial development after 1978.
Chinese infrastructure investment program (2013–) funding roads, railways, ports, and pipelines across 140+ countries; hailed as development, criticized as 'debt-trap diplomacy.'

Analytical framework arguing that underdevelopment in poor nations is not a stage of development but an ongoing result of structural exploitation by wealthy core nations — challenging modernization theory.

Framework (Wallerstein) arguing the global capitalist economy is divided into core (wealthy, high-tech), periphery (poor, resource/labor-exporting), and semi-periphery nations in a hierarchical system.
Inequality in access to internet and digital technologies between wealthy and poor nations and populations, potentially deepening existing economic inequalities.
Money sent by migrant workers to their home countries; in many developing nations, remittances exceed official development aid and constitute a significant share of GDP.
Migration of highly educated workers from developing to developed nations, depriving origin countries of human capital essential for development.
International climate agreement imposing binding greenhouse gas emission reduction targets on developed nations; the US never ratified, limiting its effectiveness.

International climate agreement requiring all nations to set 'nationally determined contributions' for emissions reductions; voluntary pledges rather than binding targets, raising concerns about adequacy.
Mass protests against the WTO ministerial conference in Seattle by labor, environmental, and development activists — landmark moment of the anti-globalization movement.

exam_tip: Unit 9 is the most likely source of continuity and change over time (CCOT) essay prompts. Practice: 'Compare economic globalization in the period 1750–1900 to the period 1990–present' — note continuities (core-periphery trade, inequality) and changes (speed, institutions, technology). For inequality arguments, use the specific data: extreme global poverty fell while within-country inequality rose — this nuance earns top scores. For climate essays, always distinguish between the scientific consensus (climate change is occurring) and the political challenge (why coordination is hard) — examiners want both. Connect Brexit and Trump to the same structural forces as the 1999 Seattle protests: globalization's losers resisting further integration.
